data.title: Global Ship Lease: A Resilient Grower With A 7.1% Dividend Yield
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Global Ship Lease Inc. (GSL) is a stock that could play an important return enhancer role in most portfolios, with limited downside due to revenue backlog and cheap valuation, and pays a generously high and relatively safe dividend. The business model is structured in a way that this shipping company is able to be both flexible and resilient in the face of abrupt changes and disruptions.

Business Overview

GSL has a more flexible fleet than other fleets because of the sizes of its vessels. It is a huge competitive advantage at a time when international trade rules and taxes are disrupted all the time. With a fleet of 69 vessels (average age 17.7 years TEU-weighted), as per the Q3 earnings report release on November 10, GSL is highly adaptable. Its predominantly smaller containerships and larger neopanamax vessels are sufficiently adaptable to its highly diversified quality charterer clients. At a time of numerous geopolitical disruptions, from the Suez Canal disruptions earlier this year due to regional tensions (Houthis, Iran) to US-China trade changes of rules and taxes, charterers are looking for adaptability first.

GSL’s revenue is well-diversified across strongly capitalised charters with a concentration in MSC, Hapag-Lloyd (OTCPK:HPGLY) and Maersk (OTCPK:AMKBY).

The revenue downside is very limited with a strong contracted revenue backlog: GSL announced $778 million in new contracted revenues in the first nine months of 2025, pushing total backlog to $1.92 billion. The company achieved 100% coverage for the remainder of 2025, 96% for 2026, and 74% for 2027, providing comfort in its financial means and the payment of its dividend being safe at least in 2026 and probably in 2027.

Hard Figures

Per the Q3 earnings report, operating revenue rose 10.7% year-over-year to $192.7 million in Q3 2025, while net income increased 17.1% to $95.0 million. The normalized EPS of $2.62 in Q3 surpassed the consensus of $2.37, demonstrating the efficiency of management. Q3 EPS is up 8.8% on a QoQ basis as well. GSL’s key competitive advantage is its low breakeven rates of just over $9,500 per day per vessel, significantly cheaper than the spot rates and further boosting the demand for its vessels.

While top and bottom lines remain strong, the company’s balance sheet is healthy despite being a capital-intensive industry in a time of high-interest rates.

The capital allocation priorities of management reside in de-risking the balance sheet and significantly reducing financial leverage, as adjusted net debt to adjusted EBITDA sits at only 0.5x, much lower than the pre-pandemic high of 8.4x in 2018. At a time of relatively high interest rates, I believe, as a shareholder myself, that it is smart for the business to continue deleveraging. The company has a well-deserved credit rating of BB+.

Given the healthy balance sheet and financial results, the Board of GSL approved an increase in the annualized dividend to $2.50 per share, a 19% jump and 67% higher compared to early 2024. The dividend is fully paid by cash flows with a comfortable payout ratio of approximately 20%. The dividend yield stands at an impressive 7.1% at the current stock price of $35 per share. The dividend yield is a reflection of how undervalued the stock is, currently trading at 3.4x trailing and forward earnings, lower than its small cap peer Danaos Corporation (DAC), trading at 3.8x with half the dividend yield.

Quite logically, Jefferies raised its price target to $39, which prices it at a multiple closer to its 5-year average of 3.7x, which I believe is a conservative price target given high dividend yields and strong tailwinds. The stock is also rated a Strong Buy by Seeking Alpha’s Quant model, thanks to its A- to A+ grades in Revisions, Momentum, Profitability and Valuation.

Risk Factors To My Case

GSL has limited downside given the contracted revenue; therefore, the main risks to consider are those that could disrupt the shipping routes in 2027 and later on: the usual ones are signs of recession but also increased geopolitical instability, as well as trade wars. However, the key mitigant is GSL’s versatile fleet with small, adaptable vessels that can quickly adjust to disruptions. Nonetheless, the stock market could rapidly price in any sign of a troubled economic outlook, geopolitical tensions, or trade wars.

Bottom Line

GSL is an excellent dividend stock, with limited downside risk until 2027 and some upside potential as the stock remains cheap. Its adaptable fleet attracts high-quality charters who look increasingly for adaptability and flexibility. Its 7.1% dividend yield is a reflection of the shareholder friendliness of management, and its pristine balance sheet ensures the business is well poised to pursue strategic investments to fuel its growth.

data.title: Ibotta: A Non-Rewarding Experience
data.description:
In the summer of 2024, I concluded that promotion player Ibotta (IBTA) was on sale itself. That conclusion remains very much valid today, with shares down by another two-thirds in the less than one and a half years that followed.

The business went public in the spring of 2024, with a promise to allow CPG brands to deliver digital promotion to consumers throughout a single network, bolstering unparalleled customizable and measurement solutions. Through the summer, shares have given up some ground amidst slower growth, but moreover, margin pressure makes it very hard to be(come) upbeat on the prospects for the shares.

By now, revenue growth has come to a complete standstill—in fact, the opposite—with heavily adjusted EBITDA metrics leaving no realistic earnings potential at all here.

Make Every Purchase Rewarding

The header above is what is Ibotta's mission, with its technology allowing consumer packaged goods brands to offer digital promotions to over 200 million consumers through the single, and convenient network. This is called the Ibotta Performance Networks (IPN), crediting consumers with billions in cash since its founding.

The basic idea behind the business and its founder, Bryan Leach, is that consumer loyalty should be rewarded, as this vision and technology is able to perform customized promotions and advertisements (unlike traditional coupons). This has created a new market opportunity, which led to the launch of the app back in 2012 already.

Such solutions offer appeal for consumers, who are always in for a bargain, certainly as they are strapped for cash by inflationary pressures in recent years. After all, consumer spending on CPG items is huge, with grocery shopping in the US alone comfortably exceeding a trillion per year.

CPG companies see benefits as well with more targeted promotions to more loyal customers, as the application can be used with pretty much all the major mainstream retailers, including names like Kroger, Walmart, Dollar General, and some more.

Sitting on a huge amount of data, Ibotta is perfectly positioned to offer the ultimate form of personalized advertising, as the technology element to this reduces a lot of friction costs in production and redeeming such coupons as well.

IPO Expectations Plunge

Ibotta went public at $88 per share in April 2024, at which valuation the enterprise valuation of the company stood at $2.4 billion, that is, if we account for net cash balances maintained at the time. Shares even briefly traded over the $100 mark as investors were upbeat on the prospects for the firm.

This enthusiasm could be explained by strong momentum in the business, after the roll-out of its services at Walmart drove a 52% increase in 2023 sales to $320 million, accompanied by an operating profit of $56 million, resulting in pro forma earnings power in excess of a dollar per share. About three-quarters of these revenues were generated from so-called redemption revenues (i.e. the cut on those), with the remainder related to advertising and related services.

First quarter sales rose by another 40% to $81 million, as operating profits were reported at $15 million. All this looked good, yet I had concerns about the strength and durability of the business model, with publishers paying a hefty cut to Ibotta in order to use its services. Moreover, typically and certainly technology-based businesses incur higher stock-based compensation expenses post their initial offering, making me cautious to get involved.

Shares fell to the $80 mark over that summer as the second quarter revenue outlook for $85 million called for just 25% growth on an annual basis, while EBITDA was seen around $21 million, down sequentially from a $22.7 million number in the first quarter. This was ahead of undoubtedly increasing stock-based compensation expenses, making me take a wait-and-see approach, certainly as I was not convinced about the strength of the business model.

Coming Down

Since my cautious take in the summer of 2024, shares have gradually come down in waves, now trading at $25, within reach of the 52-week lows at $22 per share.

The reason for this shortfall is driven by the softer performance of the business, with 2024 revenues up just 15% to $367 million, or 20% if we adjust for a break-benefits boosting 2023 sales. GAAP operating profits were cut in half to $28 million, yet non-GAAP performance showed a meaningful increase, with EBITDA reported up 35% to $112 million. However, that number was completely not indicative in my view, as stock-based compensation expenses more than tripled to $76 million.

Fourth quarter revenues were actually down nearly 2% to $98 million and change, as EBITDA margins were on the decline as well, creating a tough outlook for 2025. A first quarter guidance called for flattish revenues at $80-$84 million, with EBITDA margins seen around 15%, not too compelling either.

Buying the dip, the company announced a second buyback program in March of this year, and in May, Ibotta reported a 3% increase in first quarter sales to $84 million and change. While EBITDA was reported at $14 million and change, stock-based compensation rose to $13 million and change, implying that the business might be breaking even at best but loss-making realistically.

Another buyback program was announced in the wake of the results, and in August, Ibotta reported a 2% fall in second-quarter sales to $86 million, after initially guiding for a modest increase in sales. Adjusted EBITDA rose to nearly $18 million, with stock-based compensation being flattish at $13 million and change.

So far the good news as third quarter sales were seen down 17% to a midpoint of $81.5 million, with EBITDA seen at a midpoint of just $11.5 million. In November, Ibotta reported a 16% decline in third quarter sales to $83.3 million, and while EBITDA of $16.6 million was far stronger than guided for, realistic earnings remain minimal in between $12.6 million in stock-based compensation expenses, and some other items.

What Now?

The buyback program announced over the last year has reduced the share count to 29 million and some shares. Trading at $25 per share, the market value of the firm stands at around $725 million, this including a $223 million cash position as well.

If we exclude debt due to publishers and users, the operating asset valuations stand at just over half a billion. This means that a high single-digit sales multiple at the time of the public offering has come down to just around 1.5 times here, although that profitability has effectively turned into realistic break-even levels (or even small losses) here.

Worse, no quick avail is seen here, with fourth quarter sales seen at $80-$85 million, indicating sequential stagnation and frankly modest declines here. Adjusted EBITDA is seen between $9 and 12 million, suggesting sequential declines as well.

Right now, the fears about the softer performance of the business model seem to have become a reality here, with the situation best compared to a melting ice cube in between flattish or falling sales, with no earnings to be reported. This and continued concerns on the outlook make it very easy for me to still avoid the shares, even at these lower levels.

data.title: 451 IT Insider October: A Roundup For IT Decision-Makers
data.description:
In a world where technological advancements dictate the pace of progress, the AI revolution challenges industries to adapt or be left behind. As businesses grapple with the complexities of integrating cutting-edge AI solutions, the stakes have never been higher. This report cuts through the noise, offering a critical examination of the AI landscape, where innovation meets reality, and the path to sustainable growth is paved with both opportunities and obstacles.

The take

Global markets have reached a critical inflection point, where the rapid evolution of AI demands not only robust investment in infrastructure and talent but also a strategic recalibration of risk management practices. As adoption accelerates across businesses of all sizes, companies must balance innovation with pragmatic operational improvements to safeguard long-term value. The fundamental challenge lies in navigating a landscape marked by disruptive opportunities alongside complex integration and security hurdles.

AI in focus: Generative AI software market revenue trajectories and the bubble question

All eyes are on the AI market, with significant attention on what some view as a growing AI "bubble." The market is entering a period of intense scrutiny, which brings us to a central question: Can AI vendors build a revenue trajectory robust enough to justify AI infrastructure investments and sustain their high rates of cash burn? The October 2025 Generative AI Market Monitor & Forecast provides an opportunity to examine this question. It tracks 483 GenAI model and software vendors — specifically, companies whose core value proposition centers on GenAI capabilities, rather than those that simply incorporate GenAI features into broader products. It provides insight into vendors that offer general-purpose AI capabilities to address a wide array of use cases and customer segments and that are already generating revenue or have an active client pipeline. The GenAI industry will likely be unable to meet its proposed infrastructure investments without a significantly accelerated revenue trajectory. Aggregate revenue in the GenAI software market is projected to reach $103 billion by 2029 — a 39.3% compound annual growth rate from 2024 to 2029. However, these figures seem out of sync with studies that suggest trillions of dollars in annual revenue will be needed to fund the computing power required to meet anticipated AI demand by 2030.

Enterprise AI demand drives tech spending amid uncertainty

Intent to spend on technology among US businesses and consumers flattened during the third quarter of 2025, showing signs of recovery after a significant decline from the first quarter to the second quarter, according to 451 Research's US Tech Demand Indicator, a survey-backed composite of intent to spend on technology. US trade policy continues to affect the global economy and, by extension, organizations' IT spending plans. While the impact differs by technology category and region, we expect its influence to continue well into 2026. According to 451 Research's Macroeconomic Outlook, SME Tech Trends, AI Adoption 2025 survey, global tech spending is looking up, likely driven by multiple factors including tariffs, renewals and AI disruption. Global IT spending is expected to show a bullish outlook for early 2026, although this optimism varies significantly by region. US organizations remain notably more cautious than their international counterparts, with only 18% expecting budget increases, compared with 43% of non-US respondents. This divergence appears to be linked to tariff impacts, particularly affecting hardware-centric segments such as IT infrastructure, employee devices and the internet of things.

AI adoption continues to gain momentum across organization sizes, with 53% of small and medium-sized enterprises and 61% of large enterprises reporting usage or proof-of-concept initiatives. SMEs, on average, plan to allocate 18% of their total IT budget to AI-related spending in the next 12 months, while large enterprises project an average of 26%, indicating a substantial commitment to these technologies. The broad interest in advanced capabilities, such as GenAI and agentic AI, and the expected benefits across various use cases and departments suggest that organizations view these tools as important drivers of productivity and operational efficiency. However, implementation challenges around cost, skills and security remain significant barriers.

SMEs with an established digital transformation strategy appear better positioned to navigate market pressures, showing stronger spending intent and higher AI adoption rates. This suggests that strategic digital planning may help organizations balance external economic pressures with technology investment priorities.

Emerging tech: Exploring the adoption of a data science platform for agentic AI

Increasingly, data science platform providers seek to enable generative and predictive AI development, deployment and management in a single environment, and enterprises recognize the appeal of this approach. Slightly more than one-third (34%) of responding organizations plan to use an existing data science platform to implement generative AI in production, according to 451 Research's Voice of the Enterprise (VotE): Data Analytics, Generative AI 2024 survey. We examine the critical functionality required to effectively use a data platform as an environment for AI agent creation, deployment and management.

Agentic AI gives data science platforms fresh relevance and a stake in a high-growth market. The requisite functionality is quite expansive because it must address all phases of AI agent development, deployment and management. Data scientists and developers are familiar with data science platforms, and with this familiarity comes expectations that the same level of functionality will be available for agentic AI. Fulfilling this expectation can be hard for some players because agentic AI is outside of their core competency in data science. Organizations considering using their existing data science platform for AI agent development, deployment and management need the benefits to outweigh the challenges. The advantages of a relationship with an existing supplier include the potential to influence product development, negotiate better pricing and reduce the integration burden, while the main downside is the potential for vendor lock-in.

M&A in focus

Privately held data protection vendor Veeam Software Group GmbH is paying $1.73 billion to acquire data security posture management provider Securiti Inc., in a bid to securely enable enterprise adoption of GenAI. The buyer intends to combine its data protection suite with Securiti AI's data security offerings to enable enterprises to better leverage their data in primary and secondary storage systems, in turn supporting safe implementation of generative AI. Generative AI remains a massive prize for enterprises looking to generate content and code, or simply improve automation and productivity. However, data privacy and security risks are the top challenges that organizations cite in adopting GenAI, according to 451 Research's VotE: AI & Machine Learning, Use Cases 2025 survey. With this in mind, it makes sense for two vendors such as Veeam and Securiti AI to integrate their offerings to address enterprise risk, as the secure implementation of GenAI and safe use of enterprise data are increasingly enticing buying triggers for their joint customer base.

Notable companies

HashiCorp Inc. has launched updates to its Terraform tool and announced its plan for a graph-based agentic infrastructure platform. Since its 2020 launch of HashiCorp Cloud Platform (HCP), its suite of fully managed SaaS products, HashiCorp has worked to convince buyers that its integrated collection of tools is greater than the sum of its parts. Yet the company's flagship products — Vault (identity-based secrets management) and Terraform (infrastructure as code) — are so popular that most customers have yet to move beyond them.

Weatherford International plc (WFRD) has launched its Industrial Intelligence Platform to support digital-first energy operations. The global oil and gas industry is at an inflection point where digital transformation, automation and data integration have become indispensable for maintaining competitiveness. The new portfolio seeks to unify physical operations, legacy assets and digital intelligence in a single ecosystem. It combines the company's domain expertise with a modular suite of software and hardware tools designed to deliver actionable insight from the edge of the field to enterprise control rooms. By connecting sensors, automation systems and data management architectures, Weatherford aims to create a scalable intelligence layer that supports operators at every stage of digital maturity.

Posit Science Corp. has laid out its data science strategy, with Positron IDE and generative AI in the mix. Posit has evolved considerably over the past few years, expanding beyond R to Python-based data science by building multilingual offerings to increase its addressable market. The vendor's measured approach to generative AI is appealing because it stresses a human-in-the-loop strategy, which is critical to success. Slightly more than one-quarter (26%) of enterprises cite quality of generations as the greatest current barrier to GenAI adoption for data science or analytics, according to the VotE: Data & Analytics, Generative AI 2025 survey.

Badge is offering a new approach to strong authentication without the exchange of secrets. It has taken recent innovations in AI, machine learning and cryptography and applied them to underserved markets and use cases that require improved security and productivity. Enabling thousands or tens of thousands of users to easily and strongly authenticate to any kiosk, device or terminal helps to provide a better user experience and reduce whole classes of vulnerabilities around weak authentication or stolen credentials.

Cerebras Systems Inc. (CBRS) has raised $1.1 billion as it pivots toward cloud inference. It has developed its own wafer-scale silicon accelerators along with self-contained integrated systems and, more recently, cloud-based inference services. The series G funding comes amid a flurry of massive investments, forward-looking contract commitments and acquisitions in the AI infrastructure sector. Cloud-based inference services could be an easier route to market, as they bypass otherwise insurmountable technology packaging, customer integration support and sales distribution channel challenges that only large, established vendors can hope to address. Cerebras believes its WSE chips will continue to outperform other inference approaches. The question is whether it can move beyond offering inference services from its own hosted cloud and start attracting the big cloud providers as customers and partners.

Cisco Systems Inc. (CSCO) has unveiled AI-enabled Webex contact center solutions and industry integrations. The company introduced Webex AI Quality Management, enhancements to Webex AI Agent and updated Cisco AI Assistant features for Webex Contact Center deployments, as well as expanded integrations with Salesforce Inc. (CRM), Amazon Lex and Epic Systems Corp. These updates highlight how Cisco's previously siloed products are evolving into a unified platform, boosting value for its enterprise customers.

Attribute offers automated, granular unit economics without tagging. It is a streamlined FinOps platform positioned to "simplify the business value of cloud investments and align them with organizational priorities." It automates the discovery and grouping of cloud costs according to business metrics, crucially without the need for tagging. For granular visibility into cloud expenditures, Attribute uses extended Berkeley Packet Filter data (application runtime data for usage-based allocations) to provide enhanced views of exact costs. The company says this enables finance, engineering and product teams to better collaborate and optimize business processes to ensure that cloud investment supports business goals.

Jamocha Tech Private Ltd. (dba ProHance) aims to boost productivity with workforce management (WFM) capabilities for contact center and back-office operations. The vendor's strong workforce analytics foundation underpins its move into customer experience management. By integrating WFM features with a suite of customer experience (CX) tools including omnichannel communications, AI predictive analytics and real-time intraday management, ProHance seeks to offer a unified platform that bridges contact center and back-office operations. Its integrated approach could deliver deeper insight into contact center productivity with tools such as application-based data, as well as URL and user activity information. This could be an important differentiation for the company as it steps into an already fragmented and intensely competitive CX space.

Verint Systems Inc. (VRNT) advances CX automation, highlighting tangible business outcomes. Together with its Value Dashboards' capabilities for tracking key performance indicators, these factors differentiate its offering. By integrating its CX Open Platform with AI-driven bots and analytics tools, Verint looks to be well-positioned to meet evolving contact center automation needs. However, under new ownership, Verint must navigate challenges such as high AI project attrition rates and competitive pressures to maintain its leadership in the CX space. We believe it also needs a messaging strategy that articulates its differentiation to support its positioning as a leader in the emerging CX automation category.

Hyland Software Inc.'s Content Innovation Cloud aims to turn content into context for the "agentic enterprise." It is weaving content repositories and other sources of data into its Content Innovation Cloud. By leveraging its installed base and vertical expertise, the vendor emphasizes federation, governance and prebuilt industry-specific agent meshes to ease adoption, while open-sourcing its Cloud Content Repository to accelerate integration and ecosystem growth. The vision is compelling, but execution is complex. Enterprise-scale context and orchestration demand resilient connectors, compliance-grade semantics, cost-efficient operations and high-quality data.

What to watch

Looking ahead, the evolving landscape promises a shift in how enterprises align their strategies with new market dynamics. As organizations recalibrate to manage emerging risks and leverage untapped opportunities, we anticipate changes in operational models to emphasize agility and long-term resilience. We expect stakeholders to prioritize scalable investments and adaptable frameworks that respond to fluid market conditions, setting the stage for a future defined by innovation and strategic foresight.

These developments will play a crucial role in shaping the future of enterprise technology, offering new opportunities and challenges for businesses worldwide. S&P Global Market Intelligence 451 Research provides essential insight into the pace and extent of digital transformation across the global technology landscape, including data on adoption, innovation and disruption across the technology markets, backed by a global team of industry experts. As the enterprise technology landscape evolves, keeping an eye on these developments will be essential for organizations aiming to navigate the complexities of digital transformation.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Semtech Q3 Preview: Growth Will Last For At Least Another Year, But I'm Cautious
data.description:
Introduction

Semtech Corporation (SMTC) is due to report its Q3 earnings on the 24th of November, so I wanted to go through what to expect and what I am going to be looking out for going forward. I expect the AI growth story to continue to reward the company with a higher share price, but I am not sure how long this is going to last, and given my heavy investments in this segment, I am assigning the company a hold rating.

What to Expect

Analysts expect the company to report earnings of $0.45 and $0.23 adjusted and GAAP, respectively, on around $266.4m in sales. This translates to around 3% growth sequentially, and around 12% y/y growth. How do these numbers stack up against what the management has guided for the upcoming quarter? Quite well. The management is guiding sales to be $266m at the midpoint, which is in line with analysts. Adjusted EPS of $0.44, which is also more or less in line with estimates. If we look at the past earnings performance, we can see that the company consistently beats on top and bottom lines, although only slightly, which suggests that the management and the analysts are quite in tune, so I don’t expect to see a massive deviation in the upcoming quarter. I expect a slight beat on the top and bottom lines once again.

In short, I don’t expect to see a lot of activity since the estimates are quite close to what should be reported, unless the company provides some sort of an update on its outlook for the rest of the financial year, either to the downside or upside.

What I’m Looking For

All eyes continue to be focused on the hypergrowth business segments like the data centers and AI. The company has many products that are focused on these areas in tech, and I will be looking to see what the company is able to bring in terms of sales. The data center’s net sales growth will remain the key growth driver, which will be supported by the ramp of 800-gigabit optical components. In the previous quarter, the data center business reached a record $52.2m, which was up 92% y/y, with key products driving such performance like FiberEdge and CopperEdge platforms, as well as early revenue from the aforementioned 800-gigabit components.

I believe this business segment will remain the most promising growth driver going forward, and I will be looking out for any weaknesses over the next few quarters. The hyperscalers all announced their next year’s capex spending on data centers and AI products, and so far, it looks like there will be even more growth in this space, as all of them are pledging billions of dollars more to expanding their data center and AI infrastructure. Some of this growth will trickle down to SMTC, and I would like to get some comments regarding this segment from the management’s mouth in the upcoming reports over the next few quarters. The company recently announced advanced fiber optic transimpedance amplifiers, or TIAs for short, for 1.6 terabit AI data centers, and it will be interesting to see what kind of reception it will get going forward.

It will be important to gauge the tone of the management regarding this key growth segment.

This segment, in broad terms, is expected to see fantastic growth for the next 4 years. It is estimated that the North American hyperscale market is going to grow at a whopping 22% 5-year CAGR, driven by big tech like Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOGL)(GOOG), and Meta (META). These 4 companies alone will probably account for over $300B+ in capex spending in 2026.

The company’s products are also very big within the IoT space, so I will be looking out for what kind of performance the company managed to achieve in Q3 and what is expected to be, going forward, especially the company’s LoRa-based IoT solutions, which are foundational for IoT connectivity. This product family includes sub-GHz and 2.4Ghz transceivers and edge computing modules that are designed for much more efficient data handling. The company recently expanded its IoT device-to-cloud cellular and satellite solutions. This lets us know that the company continues to invest in high-growth, advanced tech markets.

Recently, the LoRa IoT solutions segment contributed rather solidly to the company’s overall sales performance. LoRa revenue was up 29% y/y but down 5% sequentially. I would like to see what kind of momentum this segment is going to show us over the next couple of quarters.

In general, the IoT market seems to have come back to life in terms of expected growth. It is not as hyper-growth as the data center and AI, but it is expected to grow at around a 13% CAGR through 2034. IoT is still related to AI since most of this growth is expected to come in because of the increasing integration of AI.

What does Semtech bring to the table?

Let’s dig a little deeper into why the company has performed relatively well in the past couple of years.

Semtech’s innovative solutions, like the one I mentioned before, the 800-gigabit linear-drive pluggable optics technology, are currently the market leader in this space, which helps reduce power consumption in optical transceivers by as much as 50%. How is that done? The company found a way to eliminate the power-hungry digital signal processors, enabling energy-efficient, as well as cost-efficient interconnects for hyperscale data centers and AI infrastructure. A 50% reduction is no joke, and we all know how power-hungry data centers are and how much more power-hungry they are getting, seemingly by the minute, so this sort of value proposition from Semtech is in high demand.

Of course, SMTC is not the only player in the game right now. The company doesn’t name anyone specifically as its competitors; however, in broad terms, companies like MACOM (MTSI), Arista Networks (ANET), and NXP Semiconductors (NXPI) all cover some aspects of data center connectivity, optical transceivers, and signal integrity solutions. But what they do not have is the standout product, the 800G Linear-drive Pluggable Optics that complies with the LPO Multi-Source Agreement, which is an industry agreement that specifies the standards for a new class of optical modules that are primarily used in data centers and high-speed networking. This product that helps companies save on 50% power is essential for the future scaling of data centers.

On top of this, the company holds a dominant position in transimpedance amplifiers (TIAs), which are needed for both LPOs and digital signal processors. SMTC’s portfolio supports wide data rates from 10G to 1.6T, with further R&D on 1.6T LPO drivers that are looking to be deployed in 2026 to keep ahead of the competition for the next-generation infrastructure.

The company’s FiberEdge and DirectEdge platforms are designed to provide high-performance, power efficiency, and design flexibility, which is exactly what hyperscalers are looking for. Semtech is looking to secure more design wins with major hyperscalers to expand its market share, especially now that data centers are looking to migrate from 400G to 800G and 1.6T interconnects. How big is this opportunity? It’s at least $26B by 2030, with a CAGR of 11%.

What about IoT?

I mentioned the company’s LoRa platform, which is critical for long-range connectivity and lower power consumption in IoT applications like industrial automation and drone delivery. The platform provides satellite IoT solutions, with support from strategic partnerships for expanding into private cellular networks and cloud integration with AWS IoT Core.

This is yet another differentiating factor of the company’s products. The energy efficiency, long communication range, and robustness that set SMTC apart from its peers.

In short, when you think of Semtech, think of power efficiency. The company is laser-focused on delivering the most efficient solution to the data center problem of being ridiculously power-hungry. This is going to support the company’s future growth.

How much can hyperscalers keep spending?

So, we can see that there is a lot of opportunity for the company to keep growing. But I think there is a limit to what is achievable.

A lot of companies are heavily reliant on the AI race to continue going strong, and it seems that 2026 is not going to be any different, since the hyperscalers already said they are increasing their capex spending substantially over the 2025 numbers. However, there is a lot of talk that this sort of spending is not sustainable. Companies like OpenAI (OPENAI) promise billions of dollars that they don’t have to build out new data centers to support AI, through vendor financing, essentially Nvidia (NVDA) financing the whole process by making deals with OpenAI by giving them billions of dollars, then OpenAI goes to Oracle (ORCL) to build data centers, which then buys GPUs from NVDA, closing the circle. What happens when all these hyperscalers decide to slow down? Some say the AI bubble will pop, and we will see massive multiple contractions. No company will be safe from the contraction, as right now, AI is the only growth out there that supports most of the broad indices. Let’s have a look at what kind of fair value we can come up with.

Valuation

Looking at the company’s historic performance in terms of sales and margins, I decided to be a bit more optimistic given the opportunity for the company ahead. Below you will find my assumptions for the company.

Analysts expect the company to grow its top line by mid-teens over the next few years, so I went ahead with around 15% for the next 5 years, which seems to be achievable if the massive spending continues. I am also assuming much higher margins because I believe there will be quite a bit of improvement from the next-generation offerings, which will be a lot more efficient to produce and in high demand. For the rest of the assumptions, I kept close to historical ones. Additionally, I used 10% as my discount rate, which is lower than the company’s WACC of 12%. I feel 12% is too high a margin of safety right now because the potential for the company to perform well for the next while is high, and because it wouldn’t make a difference to the overall recommendation, as you’ll see next.

So, even with such easy assumptions, the company seems to be expensive to buy right now. My model shows the company to be rather overvalued, which tells me the outlook is more than priced in.

Closing Comments

I do believe the company will continue to perform well in 2026, especially if the cash keeps rolling in from the AI growth story and the race between the hyperscalers; however, I am not looking to start a position at this point mainly because I am quite heavily weighted towards this AI growth story and am slowly starting to trim some of the top to get a little more exposure in different sectors out there, just in case this ends badly.

The company’s fair value is well below what it is currently trading at, but if you are already an investor and not looking to lower your exposure to the AI growth story, I would hold on for longer, so as not to leave money on the table.
